coredata.dc:title: A ratiometric fluorescence sensor with different responsive modes based on carbon dots-embedded Tb-MOFs for the determination of norfloxacin and levofloxacin
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Chi, Jie
Song, Yanyan
Feng, Liang
coredata.dc:description: Norfloxacin (NOR) and levofloxacin (LEV) are the two most frequently used fluoroquinolones (FQs) in clinic. Their residues seriously endanger the ecosystem and human health. Due to their similarity in structure and properties, it is urgent to develop an efficient and sensitive strategy for detection and differentiation. Herein, we synthesized a novel ratiometric fluorescent sensor for the first time by combining N, S co-doped carbon dots (CDs) and the precursors of Tb-MOFs through a facile one-pot method. The introduction of CDs effectively facilitated the energy transfer between Tb3+ and FQs, overcoming the limitation that single Tb-MOFs could not identify similar antibiotics. Specifically, the presence of NOR resulted in reverse signal response through the inner filter effect and antenna effect. The synergistic effect of these two mechanisms contributed to achieving signal amplification accompanied by a distinguishable color transition. The limit of detection (LOD) was 0.036 μM. Different from NOR, the addition of LEV reduced the electron density of the system, weakened the coordination ability of Tb3+ with LEV, and induced a single signal response with Tb3+ fluorescence intensity as a reference signal (LOD = 0.383 μM). Furthermore, the method proved to be rapid and visual, allowing for the straightforward analysis of FQs residues in water, food matrices, and biological samples with satisfactory precision. By integrating N, S-CDs@Tb-MOFs with flexible substrates, the paper-based sensor facilitated the visual quantitative determination of FQs by reading RGB values. The developed sensor presents a promising strategy for the identification and real-time monitoring of antibiotics.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluoroquinolones
Ratiometric fluorescence
Energy transfer
Real samples
Paper-based sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of N, S co-doped carbon dots (N, S-CDs)
Analysis of fluoroquinolones in real samples
Fabrication and application of paper-based sensors
Selectivity, Anti-interference ability, and stability of N, S-CDs@Tb-MOFs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of N, S-CDs. (b) The size distribution of N, S-CDs. (c) SEM image of N, S-CDs@Tb-MOFs. (d) HRTEM image of N, S-CDs@Tb-MOFs at a higher magnification. (e) FTIR spectra of Tb-MOFs and N, S-CDs@Tb-MOFs. (f) XRD patterns of Tb-MOFs (simulated, black), Tb-MOFs (experimental, green) and N, S-CDs@Tb-MOFs (blue). Inset: images of Tb-MOFs and N, S-CDs@Tb-MOFs under 365 nm ultraviolet light.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of Tb-MOFs (blue), N, S-CDs (yellow), N, S-CDs@Tb-MOFs (red) and N, S-CDs@Tb-MOFs combined with NOR (green)/LEV (cyan). Inset: CIE chromaticity coordinates of sensing materials. (b) UV–visible absorption spectrum of N, S-CDs. Inset: images of CDs solution under daylight and 365 nm UV light. (c) absorption spectra of FQs and fluorescence spectra of N, S-CDs. (d) The fluorescence decay curve of N, S-CDs with or without FQs. (e) absorption spectra of each sensing system. (f) DFT calculation of NOR and NOR-Tb3+.

(a) Fluorescence responses of N, S-CDs@Tb-MOFs toward different antibiotics. (b) The IMOFs/ICDs of N, S-CDs@Tb-MOFs in the presence of various antibiotics. (c) The corresponding fluorescence photos under 365 nm ultraviolet radiation. (d) Fluorescence spectra of N, S-CDs@Tb-MOFs dispersed in NOR with different contents. Inset: color changes of the sensing system. (e) Fluorescence spectra of N, S-CDs@Tb-MOFs dispersed in LEV with different contents. Inset: color changes of the sensing system. (f) Linear relationship plots of IMOFs/ICDs versus NOR (orange line)/LEV (blue line) concentrations. Inset: the comparison of LOD of N, S-CDs@Tb-MOFs for NOR and LEV.

(a) Preparation and visual detection process of paper-based sensor. (b) Color response of paper-based sensor with different concentrations of NOR. (c) Corresponding relationship between ΔG/B and NOR concentrations. Inset: Linear relationship of ΔG/B versus NOR concentrations.
Schematic illustration of determination and response mechanism of analytes by N, S-CDs@Tb-MOFs.
Determination of FQs in real samples.

head.ce:title: A ratiometric fluorescence sensor with different responsive modes based on carbon dots-embedded Tb-MOFs for the determination of norfloxacin and levofloxacin

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Instrumentation and Analytical Chemistry, CAS Key Laboratory of Separation Science for Analytical Chemistry, Dalian Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, 457 Zhongshan Road, Dalian, 116023, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Instrumentation and Analytical Chemistry
CAS Key Laboratory of Separation Science for Analytical Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Instrumentation and Analytical Chemistry, CAS Key Laboratory of Separation Science for Analytical Chemistry, Dalian Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, 457 Zhongshan Road, Dalian 116023, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Science, Northeastern University, Shenyang, 110819, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Science, Northeastern University, Shenyang 110819, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Norfloxacin (NOR) and levofloxacin (LEV) are the two most frequently used fluoroquinolones (FQs) in clinic. Their residues seriously endanger the ecosystem and human health. Due to their similarity in structure and properties, it is urgent to develop an efficient and sensitive strategy for detection and differentiation. Herein, we synthesized a novel ratiometric fluorescent sensor for the first time by combining N, S co-doped carbon dots (CDs) and the precursors of Tb-MOFs through a facile one-pot method. The introduction of CDs effectively facilitated the energy transfer between Tb3+ and FQs, overcoming the limitation that single Tb-MOFs could not identify similar antibiotics. Specifically, the presence of NOR resulted in reverse signal response through the inner filter effect and antenna effect. The synergistic effect of these two mechanisms contributed to achieving signal amplification accompanied by a distinguishable color transition. The limit of detection (LOD) was 0.036 μM. Different from NOR, the addition of LEV reduced the electron density of the system, weakened the coordination ability of Tb3+ with LEV, and induced a single signal response with Tb3+ fluorescence intensity as a reference signal (LOD = 0.383 μM). Furthermore, the method proved to be rapid and visual, allowing for the straightforward analysis of FQs residues in water, food matrices, and biological samples with satisfactory precision. By integrating N, S-CDs@Tb-MOFs with flexible substrates, the paper-based sensor facilitated the visual quantitative determination of FQs by reading RGB values. The developed sensor presents a promising strategy for the identification and real-time monitoring of antibiotics.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: The advancement of human society has been significantly impacted by the discovery of antibiotics. Fluoroquinolones (FQs) have become commonly used drugs to treat infectious diseases because of their broad antibacterial spectrum [1,2]. However, owing to the poor metabolism kinetics of FQs, most of them enter the environment in their unmodified form or as metabolites. The accumulation of residues can lead to the development of drug-resistant bacteria and endanger the ecosystem [3]. Among many FQs, norfloxacin (NOR) and levofloxacin (LEV) are the most frequently used antibiotics in clinic. Due to their mass production and widespread use, NOR and LEV have been detected in many environments, including wastewater, surface water, and groundwater [4]. In addition, NOR and LEV have highly similar molecular structures, which are challenging to distinguish during the detection process [5]. Hence, it is necessary to establish an effective and sensitive method to detect and discriminate FQs in complicated environments.

In recent years, ratiometric fluorescent systems have emerged as ideal optical sensors for the detection of various analytes. In contrast to conventional luminescent probes relying on single-channel readouts, ratiometric fluorescence detection offers a more reliable sensing and imaging method. This analysis technique, based on the fluorescence intensity ratio of dual-emission peaks as the output signal, offers significant advantages in optimizing the signal-to-noise ratio and enhancing detection sensitivity through built-in self-calibration functionality [6]. In addition, multiple fluorescent groups also provide the possibility to identify substances with similar structures. Ratiometric fluorescent sensors can broadly be categorized into two types based on their target-responsive signal changes [7]. One is a single-signal response with a fixed reference, where includes a reference fluorophore (maintaining an unchanged signal in the presence of analytes) and a dynamic fluorophore (the signal increases or decreases with the presence of analytes) [8]. The other type involves two reversible signals, meaning that both signals respond to the analytes and exhibit reverse changes [9]. In contrast, the second mode, with more sensitive signal changes, is particularly suitable for the quantitative determination of low-concentration targets [10].

The majority of FQs contain intrinsic fluorescence because of their large conjugated system in their chemical structure, which cannot be the foundation for differentiating this class of antibiotics [11]. However, the unique luminescent characteristics of lanthanide metal–organic frameworks (Ln-MOFs) can effectively overcome this issue. Ln-MOFs, a special class of MOFs, combine the properties of Ln3+ and MOFs materials, including large specific surface area, long fluorescence lifetimes, and high quantum yield [12]. Ln3+ can interact with FQs to change the intensity of luminescent centers through energy transfer [13]. Due to the sensitized luminescence varies greatly in different FQs, the discrimination of similar substances can be realized. Nevertheless, achieving dual-emission in Ln-MOFs faces challenges, as one common approach is to physically mix different types of Ln3+ [14,15]. Ln-MOFs based on multi-metal centers are challenging to sensitize with the same ligand due to the requirement of energy level matching, hindering the formation of dual-emission fluorescence sensors [16]. It is worth noting that the pores and channels in Ln-MOFs have highly tunable sizes and shapes, allowing them to serve as effective host frameworks to encapsulate guest materials [17]. Not all guest molecules can be encapsulated in MOFs due to limitations imposed by the size, shape, and intrinsic chemical properties of both host and guest molecules. As promising emerging fluorescent labels, carbon dots (CDs) are zero-dimensional carbon nanoparticles with an extremely small size and numerous functional groups on their surfaces [18]. These properties enhance the possibility of host-guest interaction between CDs and MOFs. Moreover, the pore-confined of MOFs can effectively avoid the aggregation of CDs, thus improving the luminescence intensity.

Inspired by the aforementioned facts, we attempted to encapsulate N, S-CDs into Tb-MOFs through a facile one-pot method to construct a novel ratiometric fluorescent sensor. The straightforward in-situ encapsulation strategy effectively stabilized the embedded CDs in the MOFs matrix and reduced the possibility of forming nanoparticles on the outer surface of MOFs. Compared with the single Tb-MOFs, the introduction of CDs effectively promoted the energy transfer between Tb3+ and FQs, and realized the distinction between NOR and LEV (Scheme 1 ). Specifically, the presence of NOR resulted in two reversible fluorescence signal changes through the synergistic effect between inner filter effect (IFE) and antenna effect (AE), accompanied by a color shift from blue to green. The limit of detection (LOD) for NOR was 0.036 μM. Different from NOR, the addition of LEV could not sensitize the luminescence of Tb3+ due to the decrease of electron density and coordination ability in the system. The single-signal response to LEV was realized by using the fluorescence intensity of Tb3+ as the reference signal (Color changed from blue to cyan). The LOD for LEV was 0.383 μM. The sensor could effectively and intuitively identify FQs with similar structures through differential responsive modes. The ratiometric fluorescent sensor with reverse signal response mode had higher sensitivity than the single signal response mode with reference. In addition, N, S-CDs@Tb-MOFs was successfully applied to determine FQs in tap water, honey and human serum samples, demonstrating excellent accuracy and precision and confirming its practical application ability. Subsequently, a suspension of N, S-CDs@Tb-MOFs was combined with a flexible substrate to create a portable paper-based sensor, and quantitative detection of FQs was performed visually by identifying the corresponding RGB values. This ratiometric fluorescence sensor developed herein offers a promising strategy for on-site detection and identification of FQs in environmental monitoring.
This section has been presented in details in the Supporting Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of N, S co-doped carbon dots (N, S-CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The synthetic method was shown in the Supporting Information.

N, S-CDs@Tb-MOFs were prepared via the following processes. Tb(NO3)3·6H2O (0.5 mmol, 226.5 mg) and 4,4′-H2bpdc (1.0 mmol, 242.3 mg) were dissolved with 25.0 mL DMF and 5.0 mL H2O. Then, N, S-CDs solution (10.3 mg mL−1, 3000 μL) was added to the mixture. After ultrasonic treatment for 10 min, the mixture was shaken at 180 rpm for 20 min to obtain a homogeneous precursor solution. The resulting mixture was placed in a 50 mL polytetrafluoroethylene-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated to 160 °C, held for 6 h and then cooled to room temperature. The product was collected by centrifugation and washed with ethanol for several times. In the end, N, S-CDs@Tb-MOFs were dried in a vacuum oven at 60 °C. The fluorescence intensity ratios of N, S-CDs@Tb-MOFs constructed by CDs materials prepared in the same batch/different batches showed almost no difference, which indicated that the material preparation had good reproducibility (Fig. S1). In addition, Tb-MOFs were synthesized according to the above method but without the addition of CDs solution.
The specific detection process was displayed in the Supporting Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of fluoroquinolones in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the practicality of the fabricated sensor, tap water taken from the lab (Dalian Institute of Chemical physics), honey purchased from local supermarkets (Dalian, China), and human serum from a healthy individual were used as actual samples for research. First, the tap water sample was centrifuged (8000 rpm, 10 min) and filtered with a 0.22 μm membrane filter to remove insoluble impurities. For the honey sample, 5 g honey was weighed and mixed with 30 mL deionized water, stirred for 30 min to form a uniform solution, then diluted to 50 mL, and filtered through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane filter. The serum sample was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 15 min and the supernatant was diluted 50 times with deionized water. The treated samples were stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C before use. The obtained solution was prepared into FQs standard solutions with different concentrations (20 μM, 40 μM, 60 μM). Afterwards, 3.0 mg sample was added to the solution, and then reacted for 1 min. The fluorescence intensity of the mixture was measured, and each sample was tested three times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and application of paper-based sensors
ce:section.ce:para: The detailed process was provided in the Supporting Information.

Tb-MOFs with good chemical stability and thermal stability are ideal candidates as host frameworks (Fig. S2a and Fig. S2b). In this contribution, we mixed the precursors of Tb-MOFs with the prefabricated CDs solution, and the MOFs framework was self-assembled around the CDs through in-situ encapsulation strategy, so that the CDs were embedded in the MOFs matrix (Scheme S1). To demonstrate the successful preparation of N, S-CDs@Tb-MOFs, the size and morphology of each sensing material were characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Fig. 1 a showed the TEM image of N, S-CDs, which were highly monodisperse and uniform with a spherical shape, and their diameters were mainly in the range of 1.5–4.5 nm (Fig. 1b). The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image (Fig. S2c) presented that CDs had obvious lattice stripes with lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) crystal plane of graphite [19]. The SEM images in Fig. 1c and S2d showed that Tb-MOFs and N, S-CDs@Tb-MOFs were both irregular nanoparticles, and their diameters were about 150–450 nm and 120–350 nm, respectively. The slight change in the size of N, S-CDs@Tb-MOFs may be due to the competitive coordination of the abundant functional groups on the surface of CDs, thereby inhibiting the growth of nanomaterials. Moreover, in order to observe N, S-CDs@Tb-MOFs more clearly, they were characterized via HRTEM. The TEM image of N, S-CDs@Tb-MOFs revealed a significant number of nanoparticles at a higher magnification (Fig. 1d). Their shape and size were consistent with those of N, S-CDs, which well-proved the effective encapsulation of CDs in Tb-MOFs. Meanwhile, the energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) in Fig. S3 demonstrated the existence of the characteristic S element of CDs, which further demonstrated the successful embedment of N, S-CDs in Tb-MOFs.

The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of Tb-MOFs and N, S-CDs@Tb-MOFs confirmed the chemical composition of the N, S-CDs@Tb-MOFs composite (Fig. 1e). For as-prepared Tb-MOFs, absorption peaks were observed at 1654 cm−1 and 1415 cm−1, indicating the successful coordination of Tb3+ with carboxyl group [20]. The band with medium strength at about 540 cm−1 was assigned to Tb–O vibrations, which further confirmed the formation of metal–organic frameworks [21]. By contrast, the N, S-CDs@Tb-MOFs had similar characteristic absorption bands with Tb-MOFs. The XRD pattern (Fig. S2e) of N, S-CDs displayed a shoulder peak at about 2θ = 20.60° and a broad diffraction peak around 2θ = 30–40°, which assigned to (002) and (100) planes of graphitic carbon [22]. According to Fig. 1f, the XRD patterns of N, S-CDs@Tb-MOFs coincided with that of the pristine Tb-MOFs, which strongly proved that the encapsulation of CDs had no impact on the main framework structure of Tb-MOFs. Moreover, as CDs materials were successfully encapsulated, the corresponding Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area and pore volume of N, S-CDs@Tb-MOFs were lower than those of Tb-MOFs (Fig. S2f).

The photoluminescence properties of sensing materials were discussed at room temperature. As presented in Fig. 2 a (blue curve), the fluorescence spectrum of MOFs had multiple emission peaks in the range of 350–650 nm. Among them, the emission peaks between 350 and 450 nm were mainly attributed to the charge transfer from organic ligands to metal ions, while the four characteristic peaks in the range of 450–650 nm belonged to the 5D4 → 7FJ (J = 6, 5, 4, 3) transition of Tb3+ [23]. A weak green luminescence of Tb-MOFs could be observed under 365 nm ultraviolet light. Tb3+ plays a very important role in distinguishing FQs. But this phenomenon was hardly reflected in Tb-MOFs. From Fig. S4, the single Tb-MOFs was used to detect NOR and LEV, a fine distinction could not be obtained.

The addition of CDs effectively activated the luminescence characteristics of Tb3+. The N, S-CDs possessed a well-distinguished emission centered at 440 nm (yellow curve of Fig. 2a). The UV–visible absorption spectra demonstrated that an absorption peak centered at 351 nm was observed (Fig. 2b), which was mainly caused by the n−π* transition due to the trapping of the excited state energy by the surface states of CDs [24], resulting in a strong blue fluorescence. In order to maximize the fluorescence performance of CDs and MOFs, two sensing materials were combined to achieve multi-fluorescence response. Interestingly, the characteristic peaks corresponding to CDs blueshifted to 395 nm in the fluorescence spectrum of N, S-CDs@Tb-MOFs (red curve of Fig. 2a). The reason may be that CDs are encapsulated in MOFs channels, which prevents the aggregation of CDs and reduces the fluorescence inner-filter effect of CDs, causing a blue shift in fluorescence [25]. The emission peaks corresponding to Tb-MOFs mostly maintained constant. Moreover, the emission peak intensity of CDs was considerably higher than that of Tb-MOFs. Therefore, the material after encapsulating CDs exhibited blue fluorescence. After adding NOR and LEV to N, S-CDs@Tb-MOFs, the sensing system displayed two different ratiometric fluorescence responsive modes (green and cyan curves in Fig. 2a). In contrast, the emission peak at 545 nm displayed the strongest reaction to NOR, and the fluorescence intensity was 12 times higher than that of the original Tb3+, which could be regarded as the characteristic output signal of Tb3+. The above results implied that a dual-mode responsive sensor integrating the optical properties of N, S-CDs and Tb-MOFs with the fluorescence intensity ratio IMOFs/ICDs as the detection index was successfully constructed.

The fundamental mechanism of fluorescence change caused by FQs was analyzed from the following viewpoints. Firstly, the XRD pattern of N, S-CDs@Tb-MOFs treated with FQs solution for 48 h was completely agreement with the original sample, which could rule out the possibility of skeleton collapse (Fig. S5a). Subsequently, to elucidate the fluorescence quenching mechanism of N, S-CDs generated by FQs, UV–vis absorption spectra of FQs and fluorescence spectra of N, S-CDs were recorded. From Fig. 2c, there was a partial overlap between the excitation spectrum of CDs and the absorption spectra of FQs, signifying the possibility of IFE or fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), which required the determination of the fluorescence lifetime of CDs before and after the reaction [26]. The fluorescence lifetimes of the sensing system with or without FQs solution were 10.34 ns (N, S-CDs), 10.04 ns (N, S-CDs + NOR) and 10.10 ns (N, S-CDs + LEV), respectively (Fig. 2d). The slight change of fluorescence lifetimes confirmed that the fluorescence quenching of N, S-CDs was mainly caused by IFE rather than FRET. Concurrently, a thorough discussion of the Tb3+ fluorescence enhancing principle activated by NOR was carried out. As illustrated in Fig. 2e, the absorption peak of the sensing system containing N, S-CDs@Tb-MOFs and NOR added the characteristic absorption peak of NOR and the intensity increased, implying that NOR including β-diketone configuration chelated with Tb3+ to form NOR-Tb3+ complexes and transferred the energy to Tb3+ through AE (Fig. S5b). Moreover, the new F 1s peak was observed in the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of N, S-CDs@Tb-MOFs after the reaction, demonstrating the formation of N, S-CDs@Tb-MOFs/FQs complex (Fig. S5c). The binding energies in the inset in Fig. S5d were 1278.6 eV and 1243.8 eV, corresponding to 3d3 and 3d5 of Tb, respectively [27]. Compared with pure sensing materials, the binding energy positions of Tb 3d shifted slightly in N, S-CDs@Tb-MOFs/FQs, which indicated that there was an interaction between the analyte and Tb3+ [28]. In order to supplement the above experiments, the coordination interaction between Tb3+ and NOR was verified by density functional theory (DFT) calculations [29]. The energy gap (ΔEg) between the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) and highest occupied molecular orbital (HUMO) of NOR was 2.05 eV, while that of NOR-Tb3+ increased to 2.36 eV (Fig. 2f). These data strongly certified that NOR had transferred energy to Tb3+. Moreover, the fluorescence lifetime of Tb-MOFs in N, S-CDs@Tb-MOFs was extended from 286.96 μs to 688.12 μs after adding NOR, which further confirmed the effective energy transfer between Tb3+ and NOR (Fig. S5e). Therefore, during the process of sensing detection, the excitation light from the CDs was re-absorbed by NOR through IFE, which reduced the fluorescence intensity of CDs, and then the NOR with strong absorption further transferred energy to Tb3+ through AE, thereby sensitizing the luminescence of Tb3+.

However, for LEV, the discrepancy in fluorescence response may be ascribed to the change in molecular structure (Fig. S5f). The substituent -O–CH2– of LEV is easy to form p-π conjugate with quinoline ring, which leads to the electron pushing effect greater than the inductive effect between carbon and oxygen, reducing the electron density of carbonyl and carboxyl groups [30]. Consequently, this phenomenon weakened the coordination ability between Tb3+ and LEV, decreased the energy transfer efficiency, and ultimately failed to sensitize the luminescence of Tb3+. Enoxacin (ENO), ciprofloxacin (CIP) and gatifloxacin (GAT) with similar structures to NOR and LEV were selected as analytes to further investigate the influence of molecular structures on the change of fluorescence intensity of N, S-CDs@Tb-MOFs. From Fig. S6a, the excitation spectra of N, S-CDs and the UV–vis absorption spectra of five analytes partially overlapped, which could reduce the fluorescence intensity of CDs. Therefore, the difference in the response of N, S-CDs@Tb-MOFs to FQs was mainly reflected in the change of fluorescence intensity of Tb3+. Similar to NOR, the addition of ENO greatly increased the fluorescence intensity of Tb3+, whereas CIP and GAT, like LEV, were unable to sensitize the luminescence of Tb3+ (Fig. S6b). Comparing the structures of the analytes (Fig. S6c) revealed that the difference of Tb3+ luminescence mainly depended on the nature of the R2 and R3 substituents. When R2 and R3 substituents induced the electron pushing effect greater than the inductive effect between carbon and oxygen, it would eventually weaken the coordination ability between the analytes and Tb3+ and reduce the energy transfer efficiency.

Since the sensor had a more sensitive response to NOR, the key factors affecting the detection performance, such as the doping amount of CDs, pH value and response time were optimized in detail with NOR as the analyte. As depicted in Fig. S7a, the different doping content of CDs had little effect on the peak position of the synthesized sensor. The ratio IMOFs@CDs + NOR/IMOFs@CDs gradually enhanced with the increase of the amount of CDs, and reached the maximum value at 3000 μL, when the addition was higher than 3000 μL, the IMOFs@CDs + NOR/IMOFs@CDs displayed a downward trend (Fig. S7b). Some studies have shown that excessive content of CDs may induce self-quenching phenomenon. Therefore, 3000 μL was selected as the optimal doping content of CDs. According to Fig. S7c, in the range of pH 4.0–10.0, the relative fluorescence intensity of N, S-CDs@Tb-MOFs remained basically constant. The results indicated that the fluorescence sensor was insensitive to pH value in this wide range. Consequently, the water environment (pH = 6.86) was selected as the subsequent experimental conditions. The response time of N, S-CDs@Tb-MOFs to NOR is also a pivotal indicator in the detection system. The influence of response time on the detection effect of the sensor was tested under the condition of adding different concentrations of NOR. Within 0–1.0 min, the detection performance of N, S-CDs@Tb-MOFs improved significantly, and the fluorescence intensity of the sensor was almost stable when the reaction time exceeded 1.0 min (Fig. S7d). Therefore, 1.0 min was finally determined as the best reaction time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity, Anti-interference ability, and stability of N, S-CDs@Tb-MOFs
ce:section.ce:para: In order to explore the specific recognition of the sensor to the target, different types of antibiotics (chloramphenicols, tetracyclines, sulfonamides, aminoglycosides, polypeptides, macrolides, β-lactams and fluoroquinolones) were used for sensing experiments [31]. The structure of these antibiotics was summarized in Fig. S8. Compared with the blank N, S-CDs@Tb-MOFs, only FQs could make the IMOFs/ICDs increased sharply with obvious color changes (Fig. 3 a–c). In particular, tetracyclines (TC) could quench the fluorescence of N, S-CDs@Tb-MOFs. In order to study whether such antibiotics could interfere with the detection of FQs, a low concentration of FQs was added to the mixed system of TC and sensor. From Fig. S9c, the sensing material could still combine with NOR to enhance the fluorescence intensity of Tb3+, and the mixed solution appeared green under 365 nm ultraviolet light. The addition of LEV could not sensitize the fluorescence of Tb3+, but due to the strong intrinsic fluorescence of FQs, the mixed solution showed the fluorescence spectrum of LEV and different color transition. The results showed that the quenching of the fluorescence intensity of the sensor by TC had no effect on the identification of FQs. As demonstrated in Figs. S9a and S9b, the effect of other interfering antibiotics on the determination could be ignored. In addition, there may be some typical interfering substances for the analysis of sophisticated samples, such as biomolecules (Glycine and Histidine), organic compounds (Glucose) and common ions (Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, NH4 +, HCO3 − and SO4 2−). As portrayed in Figs. S10a and S10b, there was no significant change in fluorescence intensity after the introduction of these interfering analytes. Subsequently, the IMOFs/ICDs was greatly enhanced after further adding FQs solution. The concentration of interfering substances was five times that of FQs in the detection process. The above results showed that the sensor had high selectivity for FQs and high concentration of interferent had little effect on the perception of target antibiotics.

Stability is an important index to evaluate the performance of the sensor [32]. Therefore, the time stability experiment of sensing materials was carried out. The fluorescence spectrum of N, S-CDs@Tb-MOFs was recorded every 5 min for 40 min. From Fig. S10c, the obtained IMOFs/ICDs showed negligible changes in eight consecutive tests. The results indicated that N, S-CDs@Tb-MOFs had good stability and great economic value in actual detection.

Under the optimal experimental conditions, the quantitative monitoring ability of N, S-CDs@Tb-MOFs for trace FQs was investigated. With the increase in the concentrations of NOR, the fluorescence intensity of CDs decreased dramatically, while the characteristic fluorescence intensity of Tb3+ increased in the same concentration range (Fig. 3d and Fig. S11a). Meanwhile, the obvious change in color from blue to green was easily recognized by naked eyes, which was validated by CIE chromaticity (Fig. S11b). The IMOFs/ICDs versus the concentration of NOR ranging from blank to 100 μM could be fitted into a linear equation (Fig. 3f). The linear regression equation was IMOFs/ICDs = 0.01578[NOR] + 0.08357 (R2 = 0.9957). Based on LOD = 3σ/slope (σ is the standard deviation of the blank solution) [8], the LOD was 0.036 μM.

In Fig. 3e and Fig. S11c, when the LEV concentration increased, the fluorescence intensity at 395 nm decreased, meanwhile the fluorescent signal of Tb3+ almost remained invariable. The fluorescence color of the sensor changed from blue to cyan, which was consistent with the CIE diagram (Fig. S11d). As depicted in Fig. 3f, the concentration of LEV versus IMOFs/ICDs displayed a good linear relationship. The SV equation was expressed as IMOFs/ICDs = 0.00148[LEV] + 0.07065. The LOD was about 0.383 μM. The LOD of the sensor for NOR was about 10.6 times that of LEV. The above experiments exhibited that the dual-signal response had better detection sensitivity than the single-signal response with reference, and the reverse change of the two signals would also produce more evident color variations. Compared with some previously reported sensors, the sensor was comparable or superior to those probes in terms of detection sensitivity for NOR (Table S1).

The ultimate objective of the designed sensor is to achieve practical applications. FQs usually exist in environmental water, food and biological samples [28]. In order to verify the viability of the sensor in actual samples, N, S-CDs@Tb-MOFs were applied to the determination of FQs in tap water, honey and serum samples. Different concentrations of FQs standard solution were added to the samples for recovery experiments. From Table 1 , the recovery rate of FQs in tap water was 90.70 %–102.49 % with the relative standard deviation (RSD) between 1.5 % and 5.2 %. The recoveries in honey sample were between 92.56 % and 111.40 %, and RSD was less than 4.7 %. The recoveries in serum sample were in acceptable ranges of 97.5 %–108.20 % with low RSDs (<4.5 %). These results fully demonstrated that the N, S-CDs@Tb-MOFs could perform stable and accurate detection of antibiotic residues in actual samples.

Instant on-site visual detection of trace antibiotics is of great practical significance. We constructed a portable paper-based sensor by combining the N, S-CDs@Tb-MOFs with flexible substrates. The carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) was adopted to modify the paper, and a large number of carboxyl groups were introduced on the surface of paper through adsorption function (Fig. 4 a). The color responses of the original filter paper and CMC-modified paper loaded with the same amount of sensing materials after dropping into the same NOR concentration were compared. The CMC-modified paper-based sensor showed an excellent large-volume response effect (Fig. S12), which proved that the carboxyl active sites were more easily combined with N, S-CDs@Tb-MOFs than the original hydroxyl group. Additionally, there was almost no difference in G/B values of paper-based sensors prepared in different batches (Fig. S13). The superior reproducibility and stability will give the paper-based sensor a promising potential in practical applications.

Fig. S14 depicted that the high selectivity of the paper-based sensor towards FQs. Since NOR could trigger a more significant color conversion, the on-site quantitative detection capability of paper-based sensors was explored with NOR as the analyte. In general, it was tough to observe subtle color changes by the naked eyes. In order to resolve this obstacle, a series of fluorescence images were taken with a camera, and the photoshop software was utilized to extract blue (B) and green (G) values corresponding to the photographs for further statistical analysis (Fig. 4b). The G/B was calculated to evaluate the color response of paper-based sensors treated with NOR. Upon exposure to different concentrations of NOR, the paper-based sensor immediately underwent a continuous fluorescence color transformation. As stated in Fig. 4c, there was a good linear relationship between ΔG/B values (ΔG/B is the G/B ratio value difference between sample and blank) and concentrations, the linear equation was: ΔG/B = 0.03894[NOR] – 0.06234, R2 = 0.9982 (blank-50 μM). The lowest LOD was approximately 0.021 μM. Table S2 and Table S3 provided Inter- and Intra-assay coefficients of variability (CVs) of the prepared paper-based sensors. The Intra-assay % CVs was 0.406–1.218, and Inter-assay % CVs was less than 5.320. All the results were within the acceptable range [33]. To sum up, the portable paper-based sensor has good repeatability and applicability, which emerges potential application value for real-time monitoring and visual analysis of antibiotic in actual samples.

In conclusion, we have successfully developed a novel ratiometric fluorescence sensor for the first time through in-situ encapsulation of blue-emitting N, S-CDs into weakly luminescent Tb-MOFs in an optimized ratio. The introduction of CDs enabled the discrimination of similar antibiotics NOR and LEV by different ratiometric fluorescence responsive modes. Mechanistic studies have shown that in the presence of NOR, the synergistic effect of analyte-triggered IFE and AE contributes to the reverse change of dual fluorescence signal, while the addition of LEV will reduce the electron density of the system, resulting in a decrease in the coordination ability of the analyte with Tb3+, so that the luminescence of Tb3+ cannot be sensitized. We validated the accuracy and practical applicability of the sensor by monitoring real samples. Additionally, we developed a portable paper-based sensor by combining N, S-CDs@Tb-MOFs with a flexible substrate. The color changes induced by the analyte were converted into RGB values using Photoshop software, enabling on-site quantitative determination of FQs. This study not only provides valuable insights for the design and construction of functional materials for the identification and detection of similar antibiotics, but also provides a promising analytical tool for the on-site quantitative determination of antibiotic residues.
Jie Chi: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Yanyan Song: Supervision. Liang Feng: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the AI S&T Program of Yulin Branch, Dalian National Laboratory For Clean Energy, CAS (Grant No. DNL-YL A202203) and DICP (Grant: DICP I202331).
